doc_title: Caddo County Natural hazard mitigation plan
text:
The Federal Emergency Management Agency
defines hazard mitigation as an action intended to
reduce repetitive losses from future natural disasters.
Counties, cities and towns are required to prepare a
hazard mitigation plan in order to qualify to receive
Hazard Mitigation Grant Program project grants in
the event of a disaster. These requirements are
found in Section 322 of the Disaster Mitigation Act of
2000 and section 409 of the Robert T. Stafford
Disaster Relief and Emergency Assistance Act (Public
Law 93-288). An excerpt of this Act follows:
Hazard Mitigation
Sec. 404. (a) In General. The President may
contribute up to 75 percent of the cost of hazard
mitigation measures which the President has
determined are cost-effective and which substantially
reduce the risk of future damage, hardship, loss, or
suffering in any area affected by a major disaster.
Such measures shall be identified following the
evaluation of natural hazards under section 409 and
shall be subject to approval by the President.
The plan was developed in an open public
involvement process that included opportunities for
the public to comment on the plan and for other
agencies and interests to be involved in the planning and incorporation of existing plans, studies, reports
and technical information. The plan is to be updated
annually and follOWing a presidential disaster
declaration.

The plan itself has these chapters: 
1. Documentation of the planning process 
2. A risk assessment that provides the factual basis for activities proposed in the strategy to reduce losses from identified hazards. 
3. A mitigation strategy that provides the blueprint for reducing the potential losses identified in the risk assessment. 
4. A plan maintenance process. 
5. Documentation that the plan has been formally adopted by the governing bodies of the jurisdictions.

doc_title: Caddo County Natural hazard mitigation plan
text:
Caddo County Hazard Mitigation Working
Group
The Caddo County Hazard Mitigation Working Group
was formed to provide gUidance during the
preparation of this Plan. This Group was comprised
of various interest groups from local government,
county commissioners, rural fire departments, and
local businesses. They identified natural hazards that
might affect the county by killing or injuring persons
or by destroying property and by disrupting activities.
The Group formulated a set of hazard
mitigation goals, which are listed in priority
order.

Hazard Mitigation Goals 1. Protection from loss of life and personal injury. 2. Protection of critical facilities and infrastructure. 3. Protection of personal property and reduction of economic injury due to hazards. 4. Minimize the costs of disaster response.

For each hazard, this question was asked: Are there
practical ways that the hazard be altered, avoided,
averted or adapted so as to mitigate its harmful
effects? Practicality was measured through
consideration of the cost of the action in conjunction
with the vulnerability, potential losses and frequency
of occurrence ofthe hazard.
The Group also identified and suggested funding
sources for hazard mitigation measure in each
vulnerable area based on input from government
agencies, consultants and citizens.
The group's meetings were designed for public
involvement to explain the basis of the State Hazard
Mitigation Plan and requirements for Caddo County.
The meeting was designed to encourage and invite
input from the State and Local officials and private
citizens.

Public Involvement 
An effort was made to solicit public input during the planning process and the public meetings were held during the formation of the Plan; one at the beginning, one after a first draft was produced, and one after a final draft was produced. Feedback received from the public proved valuable in the development of the Plan. Several comments were received that led to the re-thinking of some proposed priority mitigation actions.

doc_title: Caddo County Natural hazard mitigation plan
text:
The 13 natural hazards affecting the county have been identified as being from three categories.

1. Weather Hazards
- Flooding
  - General Rain Floods
  - Flash Floods
- Thunderstorms
  - Tornados
  - Severe Thunderstorm Winds
  - Hail
  - Lightning
- Ice/Snow Storms
- Extreme Heat
- Drought

2. Geologic Hazards
- Earthquake
- Expansive Soils

3. Technological Hazards
- Wildfires
- Dam Failure

This natural hazard identification section is to give the reader some idea of how vulnerable the residents of Caddo County are to 13 natural hazard events.

The format is:
- Identify the topic natural hazard.
- List some facts pertinent to the nature of the hazard.
- Other observations that enhance understanding of the hazard.
- List in an appendix some descriptions of hazards events that are representative of what has or could happen in the county.

doc_title: Caddo County Natural hazard mitigation plan
text:
Flooding is Oklahoma's most frequent and most costly natural hazard. Thirteen flood events were recorded in Caddo County since 1992.

Even though Caddo County is about 400 miles from the Gulf Coast, tropical systems can still create general rain flooding by causing heavy rain to fall for many hours.

During the spring and fall flash floods can result from thunderstorms.

GENERAL RAIN FLOODING

general rain flood – Accumulation of water within a water body and the overflow of excess water onto adjacent floodplain lands, characterized by a slow steady rise in stream stage and a peak flood of long duration.

floodplain – Land adjoining the channel of a river, stream or lake that is susceptible to flooding.

Flood Facts
- Flood emergencies were 71% (34) of the federal disaster declarations in Oklahoma between June 1995 and December 31, 2002.
- Few lives are lost to general rain floods, but much property is damaged.
- Standard homeowners and commercial property policies do not cover flood losses. These properties can be insured for flood losses only through the National Flood Insurance Program offered by the federal government.
- According to National Flood Insurance Program statistics, rural Caddo County residents had 2 reported losses and received payments totaling $47,328 during the years 1978 through 2001.
- Nine homes southwest of Apache that were subject to recurring flooding were acquired and demolished. This was a Hazard Mitigation Grant Program project.

HYDROLOGIC HAZARD – FLASH FLOOD

flash flood – Flood characterized by a quick water rise and short duration.

Flash Flood Facts
- Flash floods are the second greatest killer among natural hazards in Oklahoma.
- Twelve of the 13 flood events recorded in Caddo County since 1992 are characterized as flash floods.

doc_title: Caddo County Natural hazard mitigation plan
text:
Thunderstorm - A transient storm of lightning and
thunder, usually with rain and gusty winds.
Thunderstorm Facts
o The National Weather Service classifies a thunderstorm as severe if:
o Wind speeds reach 58 mph or greater
o A tornado is produced
o Surface hail of 0.75" or greater is dropped
o Severe thunderstorms occur predominately between March 15 and June 15 each year.
o Thunderstorms are equally likely in all locations.
o Thunderstorms are deadly and damaging because they produce
o Tornados
o Thunderstorm Winds
o Hail
o Lightning
o An average thunderstorm is 15 miles in diameter and lasts about 30 minutes.

doc_title: Caddo County Natural hazard mitigation plan
text:
tornado – A localized, violently destructive windstorm characterized by a funnel-shaped cloud that extends to the ground and marks the path of the greatest intensity.

Tornado Facts
- Tornados are the number one thunderstorm killer in Oklahoma.
- Tornado damage is described according to the Fujita Tornado Scale.
| Scale | Wind Speed | Damage Description |
|------|------------|-------------------|
| F0 | 40–72 mph | Light damage |
| F1 | 73–112 mph | Moderate damage |
| F2 | 113–157 mph | Considerable damage – houses destroyed |
| F3 | 158–206 mph | Severe damage |
| F4 | 207–260 mph | Devastating damage |
| F5 | 261–318 mph | Incredible damage – house slabs swept clear |
- The stronger the tornado, the more minutes a warning system has time to work. The Bridgecreek/Moore multi-vortex tornados began north of Fort Sill at 4 PM; they struck Moore at 6:12 PM.
- Timely warning of an approaching tornado can reduce casualties and injuries.
- Storm shelters and safe rooms are effective in preventing loss of life and injuries.
- National Climatic Data Center statistics storm events record 359 significant tornado days in Oklahoma since 1950. The strongest tornado on each of those days was:
* F2 - 259 days, 69%
* F3 - 82 days, 21 %
* F4 - 43 days, 9%
* F5 - 5 days, 1%
- National Climatic Data Center statistics storm events record 21 significant tornado days in Caddo County since 1950.
- On those 21 days, 32 tornados killed three persons and caused injury to 111 others and did $14 million of property damage.
- The map shows the National Severe Storms Laboratory estimates of the number of days a significant tornado will touch down within 25 miles of any location in 100 years.
- The map value for Caddo County is about 33, or 0.0168 significant tornados per square mile (33 / 25² × 3.1416, the area of a circle with a 25 mile radius).

doc_title: Caddo County Natural hazard mitigation plan
section: Why Were So Many More People killed In the Olden Days than Now?
text:
Why Were So Many More People killed In the Olden Days than Now?

The short answer is improved bUilding methods,
rescue efforts, medical attention and tornado
warnings.
At the turn of the 20th century there was no such
thing as poured concrete. It was not possible to bolt
down houses. They just sat on stone foundations,
and flew away with people in them. Modern building
codes that require better construction, but mobile
homes still offer relatively little protection.
There were also no "trauma" units, no rescue
squads, and no antibiotics. People who were
mangled and crushed developed infections,
gangrene, and tetanus. Sometimes they lingered for
weeks before death. Nowadays, if a tornado victim
survives the first day or so, it is very likely that they
will recover fully.
How homes are heated is also a factor. If a house
collapsed in the olden days, the overturned wood
cook stove might catch fire and burn the occupants
of the home to death before rescue workers even
knew they were trapped in the house. If the home
was in a city, the adjoining homes often caught fire.
Prior to the 1920's, there was no such thing as
commercial radio, and much less awareness of what
was going on over the horizon. Tornado watches are
now issued so far in advance of the actual onset of
bad weather that spotters can be mobilized, and
television weathermen geared up for a live broadcast
of a tornado in progress. Television weathermen
break into scheduled programming to point out the
exact position of a storm whether you want to know
it or not! Even at night in the middle of a raging
storm, it is still possible to keep totally aware of
whether tornadoes are any threat at all.
In order to provide an understanding of the extent of
potential damage by a tornado, the average damage
path of five tornados that caused F2 damage during
the May 3, 1999 outbreak in Oklahoma was mapped.
A diagram of the damage footprint is shown below.
The damage footprint is the outer limit of Fl
damage. The Fl damage area is 6 miles long and
has a maximum width of 1500 feet. Single family
structures and apartment units are expected to incur
a 20% loss inside the Fl level contour.
Commercial structures are expected, on average, to
be more durable than single family structures, while
mobile homes will be less durable
Inside the Fl damage area is smaller area were F2
damage occurs. This F2 damage area is 2 miles long
and has a maximum width of 750 feet, an area of .
about 160 acres or 0.25 square miles.
Applying the NSSL map value of 0.0168 significant
tornados days per square mile per 100 years to the
1,263 square mile area of Caddo County provides an
estimate of 21 significant tornados days per 100
years.
Combining the F2 damage area with the estimated
number of tornados by multiplication provides an
estimate of the probability of F2 or greater tornado
damage within the next 100 years at any location.
That probability is 0.42%.
section_path: Why Were So Many More People killed In the Olden Days than Now?

doc_title: Caddo County Natural hazard mitigation plan
section: Weather Hazard – High Thunderstorm Wind, Lightning, Hail
text:
High Thunderstorm Wind

wind – air in motion, as along the earth’s surface.

Wind Facts
- The insurance industry spent roughly $23 billion on wind-related catastrophic events nationally from 1981 to 1990.
- National Climatic Data Center storm event statistics record 104 high thunderstorm wind events in Caddo County during the 20-year period ending June 30, 2003. One person was injured in those events. Reported damage was $0.8 million.
- Buildings and houses constructed in accordance with modern building codes should withstand most straight-line winds from thunderstorms.
- Thunderstorms with damaging winds are usually part of a line of thunderstorms that can be seen on weather radar.
- The same warning system action as for tornadoes can be employed for thunderstorms as well.

Lightning

lightning – a luminous, electrical discharge in the atmosphere caused by the electric-charge separation of precipitation particles within a cumulonimbus, or thunderstorm, cloud.

Lightning Facts
- Lightning killed 88 people and injured 243 others in Oklahoma during the period 1959–1994.

Additional Lightning Information
- Secondary effects from lightning include fires, power disruption, and damage to objects struck by the flash.
- While the onset of a particular lightning strike is instantaneous, a thunderstorm cloud is always present.
- Usually a storm with cloud-to-ground lightning can be seen approaching.
- Lightning heats the surrounding air to greater than 50,000 degrees Fahrenheit. The quick heating and cooling of the air causes a shock wave that produces thunder.

Hail

hail – showery precipitation in the form of irregular pellets or balls of ice, falling from a thunderstorm.

Hail Facts
- National Climatic Data Center statistics record 1.5 inch or larger hail events in the Caddo County on 50 days during the 20 year period ending June 30, 2003.
- Hail forms when water droplets freeze and thaw as they are carried up and down in updrafts and downdrafts in thunderstorms.
- Hailstorms occur more frequently during the late spring and early summer.
- Hailstorms seldom injure people.
- Roofs, vehicles and crops are what are commonly damaged by hail.
- Hail is responsible for nearly $1 billion in damage to crops and property each year in the U.S.
section_path: Weather Hazard – High Thunderstorm Wind, Lightning, Hail

doc_title: Caddo County Natural hazard mitigation plan
text:
WEATHER HAZARD – WINTER STORM

blizzard – a heavy prolonged snowstorm, covering a wide area.

Cold Spots
- Ice storms caused by freezing rain are the greatest winter weather hazard.
- Electric power is disrupted in cities for days; in rural areas for weeks.
- Water and sewer systems must use generators to continue to function.
- Downed trees, power lines and poles obstruct roadways.
- The cost of clearing debris is huge.
- The typical major ice storm event footprint is 30 miles on either side of a 150-mile long line running from southwest to northeast covering 9000 square miles.

Additional Winter Storm Information
- The greatest danger period is December 15 to January 15.
- Almost every winter storm that visits the state leaves a bit of ice. These ordinary rain-sleet-snow events cause transportation problems, but don’t do direct damage.

Highway Icing
Every year (almost) the highways are covered with sleet, snow or ice during a winter storm. This is the primary contributing cause to a lot of traffic accidents with injuries and fatalities. It is a particular aggravation to school superintendents as they must decide on school closings for the weather, usually while the road conditions at the time the school buses would be making their routes is uncertain. It is also a big problem to all route drivers and to commuters.

doc_title: Caddo County Natural hazard mitigation plan
text:
Hot Spots
- Extreme heat is a summer phenomena.
- Extreme temperatures are slightly higher in the southwest part of the county.
- Heat stroke, heat exhaustion and heat cramps are the major human risks from exposure to extreme heat.
- Extreme heat is life threatening to elderly and frail persons who live alone.
- Extreme heat diminishes water supplies and increases fire danger.
- Peak daily electricity use spikes when people run air conditioners and fans during a heat wave.
- Roads, bridges and railroad tracks may suffer damage from extreme heat conditions.
- The highest recorded temperature in Oklahoma was 120°F at Tipton.

doc_title: Caddo County Natural hazard mitigation plan
text:
drought – an extended period of dry weather, especially one injurious to crops.

Drought Facts
- Droughts are usually associated with long periods of warm, dry summer days, but drought can occur and cause harm during all seasons.
- Drought is equally likely in all locations.
- When drought begins, the agriculture sector is the first to be affected.
- Those who rely on lakes and ground water are the last to be affected by drought.
- The most dramatic droughts occurred in the 1930s and 1950s when Oklahoma was affected by drought for years at a time.

| Type | Description |
|-----|-------------|
| Agricultural | Based on soil moisture deficiencies relative to water demands of plant life |
| Hydrologic | Based on precipitation shortfall effects on streamflows and reservoir, lake and groundwater levels |
| Meteorologic | Based on degree of dryness; actual precipitation is less than expected normal amount |
| Socioeconomic | Occurs when the demand for water exceeds supply due to a weather-related supply shortfall |

The Legacy of the 1930s Drought
The magnitude of the droughts of the 1930s, combined with the Great Depression, led to unprecedented government relief efforts.
Congressional actions in 1934 also accounted for relief expenditures of $525 million; the total cost (social, economic, and environmental) would be impossible to determine.
This era ushered in some of the first long-term programs to reduce future vulnerability to drought.
It was in these years that the Soil Conservation Service—now the Natural Resources Conservation Service—began to stress soil conservation measures.
The first soil conservation districts came into being, and demonstration projects were carried out to show the benefits of conservation practices such as terracing and contouring.
The measures continued in the years following the drought: conservation practices and irrigation increased, crop diversity increased, federal crop insurance was established, and removal of some of the most sensitive agricultural lands from production.
Other measures taken after the 1930s drought also reduced rural and urban vulnerability to drought, including new or enlarged reservoirs, improved domestic water systems, changes in farm policies, new insurance and aid programs.

doc_title: Caddo County Natural hazard mitigation plan
text:
earthquake – A vibration or movement of a part of the earth's surface, due to the faulting of rocks, to volcanic forces, etc.

Earthquake Facts
- Earthquakes have not caused significant damage in Caddo County.
- About 50 small earthquakes are located in the state each year, with only one to three of these being felt.
- Oklahoma Geological Survey’s Oklahoma Seismograph Network located 138 earthquakes in Caddo County from 1977 through 2002.
- The strongest earthquake in Caddo County since 1977 was M 2.7.
- Events are considered notable if the magnitude was greater than M5.

| Date | Mag | Latitude (deg N) | Longitude (deg W) |
|-----|-----|------------------|-------------------|
| Mar 23, 1979 | 1.8 | 35.3610 | -98.1080 |
| Sep 22, 1982 | 2.2 | 34.8940 | -98.4290 |
| May 16, 1990 | 1.6 | 35.3250 | -98.4640 |
| Oct 04, 1992 | 2.5 | 35.1990 | -98.4400 |
| Nov 22, 1993 | 1.4 | 35.2840 | -98.5380 |
| Feb 05, 1994 | 1.4 | 34.9460 | -98.1550 |
| Dec 13, 1994 | 2.1 | 34.8680 | -98.4710 |
| Apr 05, 1995 | 1.9 | 35.0500 | -98.2330 |
| Sep 26, 1996 | 1.2 | 34.9280 | -98.1750 |
| Jun 18, 2000 | 2.2 | 34.9170 | -98.1862 |
| Sep 22, 2001 | 2.2 | 35.2264 | -98.2151 |

doc_title: Caddo County Natural hazard mitigation plan
section: Geological Hazard – Expansive Soils AND Technological Hazard – Wildfire
text:
expansive soils – Soils that increase in volume as they get wet and shrink as they dry out.

Expansive Soils Facts
- Soils that expand have a high proportion of water-absorbing clay particles. Expansive soils present no problem in their natural state.
- Expansive soils are one of the nation’s most prevalent causes of damage to buildings and construction.
- Most damage occurs to roads and structures built on expansive soils.
- Water and sewer lines, gas pipelines, driveways and sidewalks are also damaged.
- Methods to mitigate expansive soil damage are well developed, although more costly initially.

Technological Hazard – Wildfire

wildfire – An open fire that spreads unconstrained through the countryside. If not controlled, wildfire will threaten property and lives.

Fire Facts
- There are three categories of wildfires:
  - Wildland fire – fuel is mainly natural vegetation.
  - Interface fire – urban/wildland fires, both vegetation and manmade fuel.
  - Prescribed fire – fire purposefully set and allowed to burn.
- Over 90% of the fires to which rural and small town fire departments respond to are wildfires.
- There are 10 rural fire departments and 8 small town fire departments in Caddo County.
- The worst outbreak of wildfires in recent Oklahoma history occurred in 1996.
- Fuel, weather and topography are the three main factors that affect wildfires.
- The particularly dangerous times of year for wildfire are late winter and late summer.
section_path: Geological Hazard – Expansive Soils AND Technological Hazard – Wildfire

doc_title: Caddo County Natural hazard mitigation plan
text:
dam – A manmade barrier, together with appurtenant structures, constructed above the natural surface of the ground for the purpose of impounding water.

Dam Safety
- There has never been a dam failure in Oklahoma.

- Causes of dam failures:
  - Excessive runoff after unusually heavy rainfall.
  - Inadequate spillway capacity, which can cause overtopping.
  - Old age and inadequate maintenance.
  - Earthquakes.

- The Oklahoma Water Resources Board coordinates a state dam safety program.
- 104 dams exist in Caddo County, of which 3 are high-hazard dams and 3 are significant hazard dams.

OWRB Dam Safety Program

The Oklahoma Water Resources Board coordinates a state dam safety program that ensures the safety of more than 4,500 dams in the state, with a priority placed on the protection of downstream life and property.

The program requires inspections every five years for low and significant hazard structures, respectively. It requires annual inspection of the state's 165 high-hazard dams, so designated due to the presence of occupied dwellings immediately downstream.

Because many of these dams are very old structures and, as a result, require periodic repair, the Water Board requires submittal and subsequent approval of plans and specifications prior to dam modifications.

Staff also coordinates periodic training sessions and workshops on dam safety issues and regulations for dam owners and engineers.

| Dam Name | Hazard | Owner | River |
|----------|--------|------|------|
| Chickasha Lake | High | City of Chickasha | Spring Creek |
| Fort Cobb | High | Bureau of Reclamation | Cobb Creek, Pond Creek |
| Southwestern Power Station No. 3 | High | Public Service Co of Okla | Leaper Creek |
| NRCS-Foot Cobb Laterals Site-101 | Significant | South Caddo Cons Dist | TR-Washita River |
| NRCS-Sugar Creek Site-025 | Significant | South Caddo Cons Dist | Kohnoosky Creek |
| NRCS-Sugar Creek Site-044 | Significant | South Caddo Cons Dist | Lost Creek |

doc_title: Caddo County Natural hazard mitigation plan
section: Frequency and Impact of Natural Hazards – Flooding, Winter Storms, Extreme Heat
text:
2.2(i) Frequency and Impact of Natural Hazards

This section contains tables that summarize the return frequency of each hazard and its impact on the county.

Hazard: Flooding
Specific hazard: General Rain Flooding
How bad can it get: 500 year flood
Expected event: 100 year flood
Where can it occur: Along rivers
How often is it likely to occur: Once in 100 years
What is the speed of onset: Hours and days
Harm to persons: People evacuated
Harm to structures: Extensive property damage
Other effects: Emergency response units unable to reach emergency scene

Hazard: Flooding
Specific hazard: Flash Floods
How bad can it get: Water covering roads and flooding buildings
Expected event: Water covering roads and flooding buildings
Where can it occur: Along any stream or drainage channel
How often is it likely to occur: Once each year
What is the speed of onset: Minutes and hours
Harm to persons: Drowning of motorist
Harm to structures: Damage to flooded buildings
Other effects: Emergency response units unable to reach emergency scene

Hazard: Winter Storms
Specific hazard: Ice/snow Accumulation
How bad can it get: Ice/snow accumulation of 2 in.
Expected event: Ice/snow accumulation of 0.5 in.
Where can it occur: Anywhere in the county
How often is it likely to occur: Once in 2 years
What is the speed of onset: Hours and days
Harm to persons: Death or injury in highway accident
Harm to structures: Electric lines down
Other effects: Power outages for days

Hazard: Extreme Heat
Specific hazard: Heat Wave
How bad can it get: 30 consecutive days of 100+ degree high temperatures
Expected event: 10 consecutive days of 100+ degree high temperatures
Where can it occur: Anywhere in the county
How often is it likely to occur: Once in 5 years
What is the speed of onset: Days
Harm to persons: Frail persons who live alone die
Harm to structures: None
Other effects: None listed
section_path: Frequency and Impact of Natural Hazards – Flooding, Winter Storms, Extreme Heat

doc_title: Caddo County Natural hazard mitigation plan
section: Frequency and Impact of Natural Hazards – Thunderstorm Hazards
text:
2.2(i) Frequency and Impact of Natural Hazards

Hazard: Thunderstorm
Specific hazard: Tornado
How bad can it get: F5 tornado
Expected event: F2 tornado
Where can it occur: Anywhere in the county
How often is it likely to occur: 21 in 100 years
What is the speed of onset: Minutes
Harm to persons: Injuries, infrequent deaths
Harm to structures: Considerable damage
Other effects: Power outages for hours

Hazard: Thunderstorm
Specific hazard: Thunderstorm Winds
How bad can it get: 130 mph wind
Expected event: 80 mph wind
Where can it occur: Anywhere in the county
How often is it likely to occur: 510 in 100 years
What is the speed of onset: Minutes
Harm to persons: Infrequent injuries
Harm to structures: Moderate damage
Other effects: Power outages for hours

Hazard: Thunderstorm
Specific hazard: Lightning
How bad can it get: Cloud to ground lightning
Expected event: Cloud to ground lightning
Where can it occur: Anywhere in the county
How often is it likely to occur: During most thunderstorms
What is the speed of onset: Instantaneous, during thunderstorm
Harm to persons: Death to persons struck
Harm to structures: Fire
Other effects: None listed

Hazard: Thunderstorm
Specific hazard: Hail
How bad can it get: 3.0 in. hail
Expected event: 1.5 in. hail
Where can it occur: Anywhere in the county
How often is it likely to occur: 250 in 100 years
What is the speed of onset: Instantaneous, during thunderstorm
Harm to persons: Injuries are unusual
Harm to structures: Moderate damage to roofs
Other effects: Severe damage to vehicles
section_path: Frequency and Impact of Natural Hazards – Thunderstorm Hazards

doc_title: Caddo County Natural hazard mitigation plan
section: Frequency and Impact of Natural Hazards – Drought, Earthquake, Expansive Soils, Dam Failure, Wildfire
text:
2.2(i) Frequency and Impact of Natural Hazards

Hazard: Drought
Specific hazard: Hydrologic Drought
How bad can it get: Dust bowl conditions of the 1930s
Expected event: Dust bowl conditions of the 1930s
Where can it occur: Anywhere in the county
How often is it likely to occur: Once in 50 years
What is the speed of onset: Months, but acknowledged in retrospect
Harm to persons: None listed
Harm to structures: None listed
Other effects: Water shortage, wind erosion, dust storm

Hazard: Earthquake
Specific hazard: Earthquake
How bad can it get: M7 earthquake
Expected event: M5 earthquake
Where can it occur: Anywhere in the county
How often is it likely to occur: Once in 10 years
What is the speed of onset: Instantaneous
Harm to persons: None listed
Harm to structures: None listed
Other effects: None listed

Hazard: Expansive Soils
Specific hazard: Expansive Soils
How bad can it get: Structures destroyed
Expected event: Structures destroyed
Where can it occur: On uplands in clay soils
How often is it likely to occur: At every drought cycle
What is the speed of onset: Continuous
Harm to persons: None listed
Harm to structures: Damage to buildings and infrastructure
Other effects: None listed

Hazard: Dam Failure
Specific hazard: Dam Failure
How bad can it get: A water supply dam could fail
Expected event: Large farm pond dam failure
Where can it occur: At any dam
How often is it likely to occur: Once in 200 years
What is the speed of onset: Hours
Harm to persons: Flooding
Harm to structures: Flooding
Other effects: None listed

Hazard: Wildfire
Specific hazard: Wildfire
How bad can it get: Thousands of acres burnt over
Expected event: Hundreds of acres burnt over
Where can it occur: In permanent vegetation areas
How often is it likely to occur: Every spring and fall
What is the speed of onset: Minutes
Harm to persons: People evacuated
Harm to structures: Houses, barns and fences burnt
Other effects: Livestock forage and animals burnt
section_path: Frequency and Impact of Natural Hazards – Drought, Earthquake, Expansive Soils, Dam Failure, Wildfire

doc_title: Caddo County Natural hazard mitigation plan
text:
2.2(ii) Vulnerability to Damage

This section describes vulnerability in terms of the types and numbers of existing and future buildings, infrastructure, and critical facilities located in hazard areas and an estimate of the potential dollar losses to vulnerable structures identified.

The estimated value of housing was prepared by from census data. Owner occupied units were assigned the median value of owner occupied housing units in the place or census tract. Renter occupied units were assigned 100 times the median value of gross rent. Vacant housing units were assumed to be either owner or rented in the same proportion as owner or rented housing units.

| Structure | Number | Estimated Value |
|----------|--------|----------------|
| Courthouse | 1 | $20 million |
| County barns | 3 | $600,000 |
| Roads and bridges | — | Not estimated |

doc_title: Caddo County Natural hazard mitigation plan
text:
The hazard area considered to be vulnerable to flooding is the area inside the boundary of a 100-year frequency flood. Roads subject to overflow during flash floods are a hazard to travelers.

| Community Name | Policies In-force | Insurance In-force |
|---|---|---|
| Caddo County | 11 | $583,000 |
| Anadarko | 7 | $479,200 |
| Apache | 49 | $1,171,800 |
| Carnegie | 3 | $89,000 |
| Fort Cobb | 2 | $145,400 |
| Hydro | 1 | $250,000 |
| County Totals | 73 | $2,718,400 |

The identity of all the buildings inside the boundary of a 100-year frequency flood boundary, as shown on the FEMA flood maps, was digitized into an ArcView map layer. That map layer was superimposed over newest available aerial photographs of Caddo County. The resulting map was visually searched for buildings. This search found located 26 homes and no other buildings within the 100-year flood boundary. Potential loss of housing due to flooding was estimated as 50% of the value.

None of Caddo County’s critical facilities are within the 100-year flood boundary. However, the county road system is subject to overflow in several places during flash floods. County commissioners have located 15 of the places. The total number of places that county roads are subject to damage during heavy rains is so large as to be practically uncountable.

| Structures | Number | Estimated Value | Damage Rate | Potential Loss |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Housing units | 26 | $1.59 million | 50% | $0.79 million |
| Other buildings | 0 | $0 | — | $0 |
| Critical facilities | 0 | $0 | — | $0 |
| Infrastructure | 0 | Not estimated | — | Not estimated |

doc_title: Caddo County Natural hazard mitigation plan
text:
Tornados

The entire county is equally vulnerable to tornado damage.

| Structures | Number | Estimated Value | Damage Rate | Potential Damage | Expected Loss (0.42%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Housing units | 7,153 | $170.1 million | 80% | $136.5 million | $572,000 |
| Other buildings | Not estimated | — | — | — | — |
| Critical facilities | 4 | $20.6 million | 20% | $4.1 million | $17,000 |
| Infrastructure | Not estimated | — | — | — | — |

Thunderstorm High Winds

The entire county is equally vulnerable to wind damage during severe thunderstorms. Severe thunderstorms are so common that the probability of damaging winds (80 mph or greater) within the next 100 years approaches 100% at any location.

Single family structures and apartments units built to current building code standards can withstand 80 mph wind with minor damage. An estimated 50% of existing housing units are built to those standards.

| Structures | Number | Estimated Value | Damage Rate | Potential Damage | Expected Loss (50%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Housing units | 7,153 | $170.1 million | 10% | $17.0 million | $8.5 million |
| Other buildings | Not estimated | — | — | — | — |
| Critical facilities | 4 | $10.2 million | 0% | $0 | $0 |
| Infrastructure | Not estimated | — | — | — | — |

Thunderstorm Hail

The entire county is equally vulnerable to hail damage during severe thunderstorms. Severe thunderstorms are so common that the probability of damaging hail (1.5 inch or greater) within the next 100 years approaches 100% at any location. Only a very small portion of buildings can withstand 1.5 inch or larger hail without damage to the roof. No estimate of potential hail damage is made.

Thunderstorm Lightning

The entire county is equally vulnerable to lightning damage during severe thunderstorms. Lightning is a deadly hazard to persons, animals and anything electric, but the damage footprint for any strike is small. No estimate of potential lightning damage is made.

doc_title: Caddo County Natural hazard mitigation plan
section: Expansive Soils, Wildfire, Winter Storm, Dam Failure, Extreme Heat, Drought, Earthquake
text:
Geological Hazard – Expansive Soils

Almost 6% of Caddo County soils have a high shrink-swell feature. Structures built on the Tillman soils, which are located in the southwest portion of Caddo County, are vulnerable, but the damage occurs gradually instead of in an event.

No estimate is made of potential dollar losses to existing buildings.

Technological Hazard – Wildfire

Almost 40% of Caddo County soils will support plants that create high fuel load for wildfire. The telltale of this soil type is the encroachment of wild juniper and cedar trees into rangeland. Structures in those areas are at greater risk to wildfire if wildfire fuel is allowed to accumulate in their vicinity. No estimate of potential wildfire damage is made.

Weather Hazard – Winter Storm

The entire county is equally vulnerable to winter storms. Winter storms down electric power lines, which can disrupt services and emergency communications at the county courthouse and county yards. Winter storms cover roads with ice, which makes travel dangerous. Buildings are seldom damaged by snow and ice. No estimate of potential winter storm damage is made.

Technological Hazard – Dam Failure

Six dams in Caddo County are classed as significant or high hazard structures. The greatest impact of the failure of the Fort Cobb dam would be the loss of the public water supply storage space. No estimate of potential damage is made.

The terms “high hazard” and “significant hazard” refer to the damage that would result from a dam break, not the integrity of the dam itself. Dams immediately above highways are considered to be hazards due to the danger to traffic on the highway immediately after a dam failure.

Weather Hazard – Extreme Heat

Extreme heat will not cause significant damage to buildings and critical facilities. It does occasionally damage roads.

Weather Hazard – Drought

Drought will not cause significant damage to buildings, infrastructure and critical facilities.

Geological Hazard – Earthquake

Caddo County has no history of earthquake damage. Earthquakes of the magnitude and intensity expected (M5) will not cause significant damage to buildings, infrastructure and critical facilities.
section_path: Expansive Soils, Wildfire, Winter Storm, Dam Failure, Extreme Heat, Drought, Earthquake

doc_title: Caddo County Natural hazard mitigation plan
text:
Current Land Use
Caddo County has a total land area of approximately
808,320 acres, or 1263 square miles. Outside
Anadarko the county would be classed as rural. Fort
Cobb reservoir and Spring Creek Lake are two water
supply reservoirs covering 4850 and 1466 acres
respectively. Transportation, urban and
miscellaneous lands total 53,770 acres leaVing
754,550 acres as agricultural rural. Of this amount
about 318,206 acres is in permanent vegetation and
436,344 acres in cropland.

Development Trends
New development is most intense in the northeast
corners of Caddo County. Housing and population
count are increasing in the city of Hinton and the
adjacent rural area. This growth continues as the
economic sway of the Oklahoma City metropolitan
area increases into Caddo County. The same
phenomenon is also occurring along the south
boundary of Caddo County economic sway of the
Lawton metropolitan area increases.
Small cities and towns have been growing slowly.
Those nearest Oklahoma City and Lawton are
increasing in land area while development density is
decreasing as fewer people live in each house and
new construction happens at the edge of the locality.
In the balance of the county, the trend is toward
larger agricultural operations and fewer houses.
The availability of domestic water from rural water
districts has made rural homes practical in areas of
Caddo County where ground water is scarce or not
drinkable.

doc_title: Caddo County Natural hazard mitigation plan
section: Hazard Mitigation Strategy – Goals and Approaches
text:
3 – HAZARD MITIGATION STRATEGY

This chapter provides the blueprint for reducing the potential losses identified in the risk assessment, based on existing authorities, policies, programs and capabilities to expand and improve these existing tools.

Implementation of hazard mitigation strategies in Caddo County are shared between the county government and the municipalities. The municipalities have primary responsibility for hazard mitigation inside their geographic areas. The county government as a subdivision of the state government has responsibility for the broad responsibility throughout the entire county, especially for actions that are not site specific, such as dissemination of storm warnings, and for tasks that overwhelm the capacity of localities, such as rescue coordination and removal of storm debris.

3.1 – Mitigation Goals
1. Protection from loss of life and personal injury.
2. Protection of critical facilities and infrastructure.
3. Protection of personal property and reduction of economic injury due to hazards.
4. Minimize the costs of disaster response.

3.2 – Mitigation Approaches
For each hazard, this question was asked:
Are there practical ways to:
— alter (act on) the hazard,
— avert (redirect) the hazard,
— adapt to (interact with) the hazard, and/or
— avoid the hazard?

Alter The Hazard – The hazard can be acted upon (altered) in such a manner as to reduce or even eliminate the frequency and intensity of the occurrence. Examples include reducing erosion sources in urban/wildland interface areas.

Avert The Hazard – This does not eliminate the hazard; it redirects the impacts of the hazard away from more vulnerable areas. There are both structural and nonstructural methods that may include building a levee to redirect water flow. A nonstructural example would be vegetation management to redirect runoff.

Adapt To The Hazard – This involves reducing vulnerability to a hazard. Examples include retrofitting structures to be more resistant to damage from hail, high winds, fires, or building safe rooms for protection from tornados.

Avoid The Hazard – Remove or do not place people and structures in risk areas. An example is to create open spaces and restrict building in areas most prone to flooding.

Practicality was measured through consideration of the cost of the action in conjunction with the vulnerability, potential losses and frequency of occurrence of the hazard.
section_path: Hazard Mitigation Strategy – Goals and Approaches

doc_title: Caddo County Natural hazard mitigation plan
section: Mitigation Actions – Flooding, Tornados, Lightning, Hail
text:
Flooding

Goal 1. Protection from loss of life and personal injury
• Warning the public of an impending flood or water over roadways
• Replace low water crossing with bridges
• Raise roads subject to flooding

Goal 2. Protection of critical facilities & infrastructure
• Flood proof critical facilities and facilities in flood hazard areas

Goal 3. Protection of personal property and reduction of economic injury due to hazard
• Floodplain management to advise builders of flood hazards and to restrict or require elevation of new structures in flood zones
• Waterway alteration
• Flood control works
• Storm sewers to carry runoff away
• Acquire and demolish or relocate existing flood prone structures
• Land acquisition
• Wetland Creation/Mitigation/Restoration


Tornados and High Thunderstorm Winds

Goal 1. Protection from loss of life and personal injury
• Warning the public of a possible tornado or severe thunderstorm winds
• Provide and publicize storm shelters in places such as schools, nursing homes, employment places and mobile home parks
• Public education on the most effective shelters
• Encourage storm shelters in homes

Goal 2. Protection of critical facilities & infrastructure
• Retrofit or protect critical facilities preventing severe weather damage

Goal 3. Protection of personal property and reduction of economic injury due to hazards
• Construction standards
• Reconstruction standards

Lightning

Goal 1. Protection from loss of life and personal injury
• Stopping outside events when cloud to ground lightning is anticipated
• Public education on the dangers of lightning

Goal 2. Protection of critical facilities & infrastructure
• Grounding of electrical equipment

Goal 3. Protection of personal property and reduction of economic injury due to hazards
• Grounding or disconnecting of electrical equipment


Hail

Goal 1. Protection of critical facilities & infrastructure
• Stronger roofs
• Protective storage for property

Goal 2. Protection of personal property and reduction of economic injury due to hazards
• Stronger roofs
• Protective storage for property
section_path: Mitigation Actions – Flooding, Tornados, Lightning, Hail

doc_title: Caddo County Natural hazard mitigation plan
section: Mitigation Actions – Winter Storm, Extreme Heat, Earthquake, Expansive Soils, Wildfire, Drought
text:
Winter Storm

Goal 1. Protection from loss of life and personal injury
• Provide water, food and shelter for those whose homes are without electric power for extended periods of time

Goal 2. Protection of critical facilities & infrastructure
• Provide backup electric power for vital services
• Underground electric lines

Goal 3. Protection of personal property and reduction of economic injury due to hazards
• Warning the public of a predicted ice/snow storm
• Provide backup electrical power for vital services
• Clear snow from highways and spread salt and sand on iced highways
• Underground electric lines


Extreme Heat

Goal 1. Protection from loss of life and personal injury
• Check on frail people during heat waves and ensure their living quarters have working air conditioning or fans

Goal 2. Protection of personal property and reduction of economic injury due to hazards
• Water rationing
• More water treatment and storage capacity

Earthquake

Goal 1. Protection of critical facilities and infrastructure
• Inspection of buildings, roads bridges and dams after earthquake event


Expansive Soils

Goal 1. Protection of critical facilities and infrastructure
• Testing of soils to determine their physical characteristics
• Designing structures to withstand the “worst possible” changing soil conditions as indicated by testing

Goal 2. Protection of personal property and reduction of economic injury due to hazard
• Testing of soils to determine their physical characteristics
• Designing structures to withstand the “worst possible” changing soil conditions as indicated by testing
• Educating building owners/occupants about the soil situation and its potential significance, especially relative to the role of water


Wildfire

Goal 1. Protection from loss of life and personal injury
• Evacuate persons whose homes are in wildfire danger

Goal 2. Protection of personal property and reduction of economic injury due to hazards
• Wildland fire suppression units
• Vegetation control
• Create defensible space
• Water supply sources for fire suppression


Drought (Hydrologic)

Goal 1. Protection of personal property and reduction of economic injury due to hazards
• Use historic low rainfall in water supply planning
• Develop multiple water supplies
• Develop additional water supply
• Water rationing
• Cloud seeding


All Hazards

Goal 1. Minimize the costs of disaster response
• Public awareness and education programs
• Mitigation plans
section_path: Mitigation Actions – Winter Storm, Extreme Heat, Earthquake, Expansive Soils, Wildfire, Drought

doc_title: Caddo County Natural hazard mitigation plan
section: Mitigation Strategy – Action Items (Flooding, Tornados, Ice Storm)
text:
3.3 – The Mitigation Strategy: Action Items

This section identifies specific actions to achieve these goals, an appropriate lead person for each action, a relative schedule, and funding sources. The Caddo County Hazard Mitigation Planning Committee reviewed the list of practical actions and projects. Those listed below were determined to be those most practical and beneficial in hazard reduction to Caddo County.

Flooding items:

Action Item #1 – Develop baseline for existing development in flood plains.
1. Obtain higher resolution, color aerial photographs of county
2. Locate buildings and facilities inside 100-year flood boundary using photos with flood boundary overlay
3. Provide findings to create baseline for flood plain development
4. Provide findings and map sheets to flood plain management board

Responsibility: Association South Central Oklahoma Governments (ASCOG), staff time (ASCOG)  
Budget: Aerial photographs $35,000

Action Item #2 – County Floodplain Board
1. Staff and facility support of Caddo County Floodplain Board (This board reviews proposed building sites to determine if the new residential dwellings in the Special Flood Hazard Area have their lowest floor at or above the base flood elevation.)

Responsibility: Caddo County Emergency Management  
Budget: Staff time (operating funds)

Action Item #3 – Integration of E-911 addressing with floodplain management
Explanation: In areas served by E-911 use of request for new address for a new building to evaluation of that address location to trigger review by Floodplain Board of location in the Special Flood Hazard Area.

Responsibility: E-911 administrators  
Budget: Staff time (operating funds)

Action Item #4 – Flood warnings, barricades and evacuation
1. Warn residents in flood areas of impending flood and evacuation if appropriate
2. Temporary barricades of low water crossings during flash floods

Responsibility: Caddo County Commissioners  
Budget: Staff time (operating funds)


Tornados and High Thunderstorm Wind items:

Action Item #5 – Tornado and severe thunderstorm weather watch and warnings
1. Maintaining operation of emergency management office during periods of forecasted severe weather to receive reports of severe weather
2. Coordinating training, deployment and reporting of storm watchers
3. Relay of reports of impending severe weather to local law enforcement agencies

Responsibility: Caddo County Emergency Management  
Budget: Staff time (operating funds)


Ice Storm items:

Action Item #6 – Generators for emergency electric power
1. Identify priority locations

Responsibility: Caddo County city and town governments  
Budget: Grants or federal property loan
section_path: Mitigation Strategy – Action Items (Flooding, Tornados, Ice Storm)

doc_title: Caddo County Natural hazard mitigation plan
section: Mitigation Strategy – Action Items (Wildfire and Earthquake and Other)
text:
Wildfire items:

Action Item #6 – Rural Fire Department Support
1. Responding with county equipment during fire emergencies
2. Helping small fire departments seek and secure sales tax funding and grants
3. Encouragement of county fire association (This group is to be a forum for after-action review of events requiring mutual aid.)

Responsibility: Caddo County  
Budget: Staff time (operating funds)

Action Item #7 – Controlled burn association
1. Affiliation of landowners for the purpose of conducting controlled burns of brush cover land to reduce fuel load during periods of drought
2. Furnish expertise, training and equipment for controlled burns

Responsibility: ASCOG RCD  
Budget: Staff time (operating funds)


Earthquake items:

Action Item #8 – Inspection of damage after earthquake
1. Build list of buildings, roads bridges and dams to be inspected for damage immediately after a strong earthquake

Responsibility: Caddo County Emergency Management  
Budget: Staff time (operating funds)

Action Item #9 – Inspection of conservation district flood control dams
1. Make available annual inspection reports, conducted by the three conservation districts, to Caddo County Emergency Management

Responsibility: Caddo County Conservation District(s)  
Budget: Staff time (operating funds)

Other items:

Action Item #12 – Hazard mitigation and response committee
1. Create a committee made up of city-county-private citizens specifically charged with the responsibility of plan maintenance and solicitation of public input

Responsibility: Caddo County Emergency Management  
Budget: Staff time (operating funds)
section_path: Mitigation Strategy – Action Items (Wildfire and Earthquake and Other)

doc_title: Caddo County Natural hazard mitigation plan
section: Mitigation Strategy – Committee and Plan Maintenance
text:
4 – PLAN MAINTENANCE

4.1 – Maintenance Schedule

Caddo County will ensure that regular review and update of the Hazard Mitigation Plan occurs. The Caddo County Hazard Mitigation Plan Evaluation Committee consists of members from the local elected officials and other concerned parties, including the County Office of Emergency Management, the Association of South Central Oklahoma Governments and the general public. The Plan Evaluation Committee is responsible for contacting committee members and organizing the annual meeting. The meeting will be held in June of each year, and committee members will be responsible for monitoring and evaluating the progress of the mitigation strategies in the Plan.

The committee will review each goal and objective to determine their relevance to changing situations in the County, as well as changes in State or federal policy, and to ensure that they are addressing current and expected conditions. The committee will also review the risk assessment portion of the Plan to determine if this information should be updated or modified. The parties responsible for the implementation actions will report on the status of their projects and will include which implementation process worked well, any problems encountered, how coordination efforts were proceeding, and which strategies should be revised.

The Planning committee will then have three months to update and make changes to the Plan before submitting it to the Committee members and the State Hazard Mitigation Officer. If changes are necessary, the State Hazard Mitigation Officer will be given a justification for this determination.


4.2 – Continued Public Involvement

Caddo County is dedicated to involving the public in the continual reshaping and updating of the Hazard Mitigation Plan. The Hazard Mitigation Plan Evaluation Committee members are responsible for the annual review and update of the Plan. Although they represent the public to some extent, the public will be able to directly comment on and provide feedback about the Plan.

Copies of the Plan will be cataloged and kept on hand at all of the public libraries in the County. The Plan will also be available by Website. In addition, copies of the plan and any proposed changes will be posted on the ASCOG website. This website will also contain an email address and phone number to which people can direct their comments or concerns.

A public meeting will also be held after each annual Mitigation Plan Evaluation Committee meeting. This will provide the public a forum for which they can express its concerns, opinions, or ideas about the Plan. The Caddo County Commissioners will publicize and host this meeting.
section_path: Mitigation Strategy – Committee and Plan Maintenance

doc_title: 14d60a3ca9__Bryan
section: Flood Control Dams in Bryan County
text:
Oklahoma has 2,107 flood control dams in 61 counties.
These dams have been constructed through local
watershed project sponsors with financial and technical
assistance from the USDA Natural Resources
Conservation Service (NRCS) authorized through Public
Law 78-534 (Washita River Watershed) and Public Law
83-566 Watershed Protection and Flood Prevention
Program. One of these dams is in Bryan County.
The primary purpose of flood control dams is to reduce
flooding. The secondary benefits of the dams address a
myriad of public needs such as water supply, water
quality, soil health, water management, wetland
enhancement, fish and wildlife habitat, and recreation.
Flood control dams improve public safety, contribute to a
healthy economy and support a strong nation.
Watershed projects also include the installation of
natural resource conservation practices such as
terraces, waterways, ponds, gully repair, and pasture
and rangeland plantings. These conservation practices
improve water quality and soil health and reduce
sedimentation into the lakes formed by the dams.
Operation and Maintenance of Dams
The annual operation and maintenance of dams is the
responsibility of project sponsors (local units of
governments such as conservation districts).
Operation is the administrative and management
activities necessary to ensure the dams function as
designed and remain safe. Operation work includes
annual dam inspections and inspection immediately
following heavy rains.
Maintenance work includes removing trees from dams
and spillways, repairing erosion damage, repairing
damage to the spillway and dams after heavy
rainstorms, and keeping the principal spillway inlet
towers cleared of debris.
Operation and Maintenance Needs
Operation and maintenance of dams can be expensive
and labor intensive. $4 million is needed to operate and
maintain all 2,107 flood control each year. Only through
continued investment in operation and maintenance will
future generations enjoy the promise of safety these
dams offer.
section_path: Flood Control Dams in Bryan County

doc_title: 14d60a3ca9__Bryan
text:
Annual Benefits
The 2,107 flood control dams and conservation practices
in watershed projects provide $91 million in average
annual benefits. The table on the back of this page lists
the annual benefits provided by watershed projects in
Bryan County.

Rehabilitation and Dam Safety
As dams age some will need rehabilitation to remain
safe and protect the people that live or work
downstream.
At the conclusion of 2016, 260 flood control dams in the
state have been classified as high hazard. Of these 115
do not meet current state or federal safety criteria.
Approximately $300 million is needed to upgrade the
115 dams.
The dam in Bryan County is not classified as high
hazard.
The number of high hazard dams will continue to
increase as long as residential and business
development is allowed downstream of the dam in the
breach flood area.
NRCS can provide 65 percent of the rehabilitation costs
and technical assistance to rehabilitate high hazard
dams. Local project sponsors provide 35 percent of the
cost and obtain any needed additional land rights.
As of December 2016 thirty-five dams in the state have
been rehabilitated and 18 others are in various stages of
planning, design or construction.

doc_title: 14d60a3ca9__Bryan
text:

- Name: Lower Clear Boggy Ck.

Infrastructure & Agriculture Benefits:
